title: Hundreds of thousands of Aussies to get pay boost after landmark ruling
text:
Young Australian workers ﻿are in line for a pay rise after the Fair Work Commission handed down a landmark decision on discounted junior rates.

The FWC ruled to remove junior rates for workers aged 18 to 20 who have at least six months' service in retail, fast food and pharmacy.

The discounted rates in the retail, fast food and pharmacy awards will be phased out over the next four years.

Young Australian workers ﻿are in line for a pay rise following a landmark Fair Work Commission decision. (Nikki Short/SMH)

The decision follows a long-running "adult age, adult wage" campaign run by the Shop, Distributive and Allied Employees Association (SDA).

Workers under the age of 21 in retail, fast food and pharmacy are currently paid a percentage of the adult rate of pay.

The pay rates include 70 per cent for an 18-year-old, 80 per cent for a 19-year-old and 90 per cent for a 20-year-old.

During its campaign, the retail, fast food and warehousing union also pushed to lift the pay rates of 16 and 17-year-olds but the FWC rejected this proposal.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers said the decision to lift rates for workers over 18 as a "great outcome for young workers" in retail, fast food and pharmacy.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers welcomed the decision. (Alex Ellinghausen)

﻿"The independent Fair Work Commission decided to get rid of junior pay rates for people over 18 in these industries, but with phasing periods for businesses to adjust," he said.

"﻿It's one of those decisions that recognises there was an unfairness here when it came to younger workers who were over 18 in those industries, but also providing for a sensible phasing period for businesses to adjust to the new arrangements.

"This is all about ensuring that Australians get fair and decent wages.﻿

"The outcome announced by the Fair Work Commission will help achieve that."

SDA national secretary Gerard Dwyer said the ruling was a "landmark decision, up there with the introduction of equal pay for women in the 1970s".

"It may take longer than we would have liked, but the principle has been established that no longer will 18-year-olds be treated as second-class citizens," Dwyer said.

"Their work is as valuable as anyone else's and before too long they will be paid accordingly."

title: Jackie O files $82.25 million lawsuit after radio contract torn up
text:
Radio host Jackie O has filed ﻿legal proceedings against radio network ARN, claiming compensation of at least $82.25 million and alleging that her termination was against the Fair Work Act.

In a notice to shareholders this morning, ARN said ﻿it disputed Jackie "O" Henderson's claims and will defend against the suit in court.

It is the latest salvo in the ongoing feud between the network and its once highest-rating program, the Kyle and Jackie O show﻿.

LIVE UPDATES: UN calls emergency meeting over deaths of peacekeepers ﻿

Jackie 'O' Henderson has launched legal action. (WireImage)

The duo, who dominated Sydney radio ratings for years, saw their audience fall in a much-touted move to Melbourne more recently.

The co-hosts fell out after an on-air argument on February 20, after which Henderson reportedly declared she was unable to work with Sandilands.

The co-hosts fell out after an on-air argument on February 20. (Nine)

Both of their contracts were ultimately torn up and the show was cancelled.

In its statement to shareholders, ARN said Henderson also accused the company﻿ of making "misleading and deceptive" market statements, a claim ARN also denies.

The proceedings have been filed in the Federal Court, ﻿where Sandilands' own lawsuit against his former employer was first heard last Friday.

Sandilands revealed his contract had been terminated on March 18, less than an hour before ARN Media confirmed the news in a market announcement published on the Australian Stock Exchange.

Sandilands and Henderson have been on air together for more than 25 years. (Instagram/Jackie O)

"ARN Media advises that it has issued a notice of termination of contract to Mr Kyle Sandilands and Quasar Media Services Pty Ltd," the statement said.

"As a result, the Kyle and Jackie O show will no longer be presented.﻿"

Sandilands said he had apologised to Henderson the night after the on-air argument but claimed ARN had used the rift to "burn the place down".

Details of the on-air blow-up that derailed Kyle and Jackie O's $200 million deal

﻿The on-air fight began after Jackie O read from Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor's astrology birth chart and Sandilands complained about her interest in astrology charts.

"Your fixation on this has made you almost unworkable," Sandilands told her.

"You are off with the fairies with this s---, it's mental."

Jackie O Henderson on Her Best Life (Spotify/ Her Best Life)

He claimed her fixation on astrology charts made her sound like a "drug addict".

Henderson has previously opened up on air about addiction issues that led to her going to rehabilitation in the US in 2022.

Henderson said Sandiland's accusations were "so unfair" and that he was attacking her on air.

"I would never say things like that about you," she said.

Kyle Sandilands says he has a "mortgage to pay like everyone else" as he chases $85 million from his former employer in the Federal Court. (A Current Affair)

"It's a total attack, it really is. Kyle, to say 'you don't know what the F is going on, you're not doing your job,' that's an attack and I wouldn't do that."

Sandilands and Henderson have been in an on-air partnership for 27 years.

They moved to breakfast in 2005 and then to KIIS in 2014.

They have been under contract in a lucrative $200 million, 10-year deal that was set to run until December 2034.﻿﻿

Full statement from ARN Media regarding Jackie O's lawsuit

ARN Media Limited [ASX: A1N] announces that Ms Jacqueline Henderson and her related company have commenced legal proceedings against ARN.

The proceedings have been ﬁled in the Federal Court against Commonwealth Broadcasting Corporation Pty Ltd (CBC), a subsidiary of ARN which is the licence holder for KIIS 1065 Sydney and contracted with Ms Henderson and her services company. Unsealed copies of Court documents in respect of the proceedings were served on CBC on 30 March 2026 after market close.

In summary, the applicants claim that the termination of Ms Henderson's contract constituted adverse action. Ms Henderson sent a 'Complaint Letter' to CBC which noted that Ms Henderson "cannot continue to work with Mr Kyle Sandilands" and made psychosocial health and safety and bullying complaints in relation to the conduct of Mr Kyle Sandilands on and prior to 20 February 2026. It is alleged that the Complaint Letter involved the exercise or proposal to exercise workplace rights, and that the contract was terminated because of that exercise or proposed exercise, in alleged contravention of section 340 of the Fair Work Act 2009 (Cth). It is also alleged the termination of her contract amounted to a repudiation of that agreement.

The applicants also claim that ARN's ASX Market announcement of 3 March 2026 included misleading and deceptive statements under the Australian Consumer Law. The applicants claim compensation of 'at least $82,250,000', and payment of a pecuniary penalty, plus interest and costs.

ARN disputes the claims. It intends to defend the proceedings. Given the early stage of the matter, ARN is unable to reliably estimate the outcome or any potential ﬁnancial impact.

title: Card surcharge ban 'doesn't pass the pub test', AHA boss says
text:
The Reserve Bank has announced an end to surcharges on bank card payments, as cash usage continues to dwindle - but not everybody is happy about it.

The RBA's new paper, published following an extensive consultation with s﻿takeholders, found that surcharging on debit, pre-paid, and credit cards should end on eftpos, Mastercard, and Visa networks.

"The surcharging framework, introduced more than two decades ago, is no longer achieving its intended purpose of steering consumers towards making more efficient payment choices," the RBA said in a release.

LIVE UPDATES: UN calls emergency meeting over deaths of peacekeepers ﻿

The RBA will end card use fees later this year. (Getty)

"The increased prevalence of businesses surcharging all cards at the same rate, challenges with enforcing the current surcharging framework, and consumers using less cash have reduced the effectiveness of the surcharging regime."

The paper found removing surcharges would make card payments simpler and more transparent, while also increasing competition among payment service providers.

"Removing surcharging also aligns with the preference of most consumers for payment costs to be incorporated into advertised prices," the release said.

However, the Australian Hotels Association says the decision lets down both consumers and businesses.

"The RBA's decision today doesn't pass the pub test - instead of backing consumers and small businesses, the RBA has backed the banks, Visa, Mastercard and payments companies," chief executive Stephen Ferguson said.

"It's all smoke and mirrors - consumers and small businesses have been abandoned in the middle of a cost-of-living crisis."

Ferguson said the RBA confirmed the total cost of a coffee or beer won't decrease as a result of its decision.

"What was the purpose of the whole exercise if it wasn't to decrease costs for consumers?" he said.

"Worse still, even if you pay cash now, they'll still have their hands in your pocket thanks to today's decision. People using their own money will still be subsidising the cost of those who borrow money."﻿

Other changes coming into force ﻿

The RBA will also lower the caps on interchange fees paid by Australian businesses.

"These changes are expected to lower businesses' costs when they accept domestic or overseas card payments," the RBA release said.

"Small businesses should benefit the most because they tend to pay fees closer to the existing caps."

A future public consultation will focus on retail payments such as mobile wallets. (Getty)

Transparency will also increase over fees charged by card networks and payment service providers.

"Improving transparency will enhance competition between players within the payments chain, put downward pressure on card payment costs and make it easier for businesses to shop around for a better deal," the release said.

Card surcharges will end on October 1, 2026, while the reduction of the interchange caps for domestic card transactions will come into force on the same day.

The introduction of an interchange cap on foreign cards and some changes to payment cost transparency will come into effect later, on April 1, 2027, as the RBA said more time was needed for the payments industry to make these more "complex" changes.

A new public consultation will begin mid-year to look into other areas of the retail payments system, including mobile wallets, three-party card networks, "buy now pay later" services, and e-commerce platforms.

title: Study confirms parents' worst fears: vaping causes cancer
text:
Nicotine vapes and e-cigarettes have been linked to deadly cancers , according to a groundbreaking new Australian study.

The study from the University of New South Wales, published today in the journal Carcinogenesis ﻿, found after a global analysis that nicotine-based vapes are likely to cause lung and oral cancers.

﻿The review says that while researchers have long focused on vaping as a gateway to smoking, less attention has been paid to whether the devices might cause cancer on their own.

Vaping has been linked to lung cancer and oral cancer. (Getty)

"Considering all the findings – from clinical monitoring, animal studies and mechanistic data – e-cigarettes are likely to cause lung cancer and oral cancer," lead researcher Adjunct Professor Bernard Stewart said.

He said the consistency of findings across those disciplines was striking, but the exact number of attributable cancer cases remained unclear.

"Our assessment is qualitative and does not involve a numerical estimate of cancer risk or burden," he said.

READ MORE: Inside the police operation that killed Dezi Freeman ﻿

Warnings about the health risks of tobacco were ignored for decades. (Getty Images/iStockphoto)

"We'll only be able to determine the precise risk once longer-term studies are available."

Laws to regulate vapes were introduced in Australia in 2023, including a ban on disposable and non-therapeutic devices.

Therapeutic vapes are only legally sold in pharmacies, and only to help people quit smoking - though Stewart said vapes also served as a gateway to smoking tobacco.

Disposable and non-therapeutic vapes are banned in Australia. (9News)

A thriving black market has also seen vapes sold to buyers throughout the country, including to young people.﻿

Study co-author Associate Professor Freddy Sitas also said ﻿there was growing evidence that "therapeutic" users were not necessarily successful in quitting cigarettes.

"Most of those who use e-cigarettes to quit smoking end up in 'dual-use-limbo', unable to shake off either habit," he said.

"What we do know from recent epidemiological evidence from the USA is that those who both vape and smoke are at an additional four-fold increased risk of developing lung cancer."

Sitas and Stewart warned against delaying action on vapes, saying early warning signs of lethal health risks were similarly ignored over smoking for nearly a century.

"Early reports linked smoking to infectious diseases such as tuberculosis, followed by cardiovascular disease, stroke and lung cancer," Sitas said.

"E-cigarettes were introduced about 20 years ago. We should not wait another 80 years to decide what to do."

title: US Army investigating Kid Rock helicopter stunt
text:
The US Army is investigating after a helicopter was filmed performing a low-altitude manoeuvre outside the home of musician Kid Rock while he saluted.

The singer posted a video of the Apache attack helicopter hovering above his Nashville home while he saluted.

"This is a level of respect that s— for brains Governor of California will never know," he captioned the video on Instagram.

READ MORE: Donald Trump to put his name on all US paper money ﻿

Kid Rock in the Oval Office with Donald Trump. (AP)

"God Bless America and all those who have made the ultimate sacrifice to defend her."

Now the US Army has ordered an investigation as to why the Apache was flying so close to the singer's house.

"Fort Campbell leadership is aware of a video circulating on social media depicting AH-64 Apache helicopters operating in the vicinity of a private residence associated with Mr. Robert Ritchie (also known as 'Kid Rock')," a statement read.

"The command has initiated an investigation to review the circumstances surrounding this activity."

Kid Rock performing on the final day of the the Republican National Convention on Thursday, July 18, 2024, in Milwaukee. (AP)

Kid Rock 's Nashville home is styled after the White House.

The singer has become one of the few celebrities to still associate himself with Donald Trump .

An early progenitor of the country-rap genre, Kid Rock was particularly successful in the United States . Several of his albums also made the charts in Australia.

The Army is also being asked about why military helicopters flew low over a crowd of anti-Trump protesters that same day.

An Apache helicopter flies about the Nashville No Kings protest. (AP)

The No Kings crowd in Nashville was buzzed by two Apaches. It is not apparent if it is the same helicopter in Kid Rock's video.

"Our pilots do regularly fly routes outside the Fort Campbell area," the Army spokesperson said.

"We just don't know if it was incidental or if it was deliberate."

Thousands of people took to the streets for the protest in Nashville , one of countless rallies across the US.

Organisers said at least eight million people took place in the demonstrations, largely protesting the Trump presidency .

Thousands took to the streets in Nashville for the No Kings protest. (AP)

﻿

title: When will fuel prices in Australia go down?
text:
Australians are set to see some much-needed relief at the petrol pump after Anthony Albanese announced the reduction of a key fuel tax for three months.

The fuel excise, a tax on petrol, will be halved, seeing petrol prices finally go down after soaring for weeks due to the supply issues arising from the Middle East conflict .

These changes and others will take effect soon, with Australians now having more certainty as to when they will pay less at the bowser.

Anthony Albanese announced the fuel excise would be temporarily halved, providing much-needed relief at the petrol pump for Australians. (Alex Ellinghausen)

When will petrol prices go down?

The fuel excise cut will go into effect tomorrow on April 1.

Given it will be signed into law, it means it will have an instant impact on the cost of fuel, with Albanese claiming it would reduce the cost of petrol by 26.3 cents per litre.

But - stations will have to first sell the batch of fuel they had paid for at the full rate of fuel excise.

So on April 1﻿ the changes may not be immediate, unless you are filling up at a station that has a high turnover of fuel, and a new batch taxed at the half rate has already been bought and filled.

Treasurer Jim Chalmers elaborated and said filling up a 65l tank with petrol would be $19 cheaper once the changes happen.

According to the latest figures released by the Australian Institute of Petroleum , unleaded petrol costs around $2.53 a litre, with diesel well over $3 a litre, which is why farmers and heavy transport industries are being hit particularly hard.

A further measure of relief is the temporary halt of the heavy road vehicle user charge, which hits truckies with a 32.6 cents per litre charge on diesel.

This will also be in place for three months.

READ MORE: Why thousands of Aussies plan to cancel their health insurance tomorrow

The benefits will be seen as soon as Wednesday. (Getty)

Why is petrol so expensive right now?

Longer term, the price of oil will continue to keep petrol prices high, and that is something out of the governments control.

Crude oil currently costs around $115 a barrel; it was sitting in and around the $65 region before the Middle East conflict broke out and impacted the Strait of Hormuz, a vital shipping lane in the region for worldwide oil trade.

Not only is oil more expensive, the volatile situation in the Middle East means it is harder for oil to get to Australia, even though the government insists supply isn't a longer-term issue at the moment.

With increased demand, more people are rushing to fill up - some even hoarding petrol - which is leading to shortages and more pain at the pump for Australians in the cities and in the rural areas.

READ MORE: Why NSW government has decided not to offer free public transport for commuters amid fuel crisis

Are petrol stations running out of fuel?

Hundreds of petrol stations across Australia have reported running out of petrol or diesel at different points in the last two weeks.

It is difficult to ascertain the true levels, though states and territories often release daily figures of how many petrol stations are out of fuel.

Some petrol stations run out of both petrol and diesel, though some only run out of one or the other.

For example in New South Wales last weekend, 66 petrol stations were out of fuel.

However, Transport Minister John Graham said some regional areas that were low on fuel previously had been resupplied.

Petrol bowsers across the country have been running out of fuel. (Nine)

How long will these petrol prices last?

As mentioned before, the only way we will see prolonged relief at the pump will be when the price of oil drops.

That almost exclusively hinges on the fate of the Middle East conflict, which shows no signs of slowing anytime soon.

NSW Premier Chris Minns cautioned his state to prepare for long-term disruption as a result of the war.

"I am not seeing any indication that this will be resolved in 30 days," Minns said.

"I don't know whether it is going to be over in a week, or whether it's going to be a prolonged situation... but we have got to prepare for every eventuality."

How many days of petrol does Australia have left?

Energy Minister Chris Bowen insisted Australia still has a month of supply of petrol , and said shipments are still coming in despite some cancellations.

Briefing parliament yesterday, he said the country has nine days' worth of petrol, 30 days' worth of diesel; and 30 days' worth of jet fuel.

﻿Bowen said any shipments that were cancelled have been more than properly replaced, as he looks to ease fears of a drop in supply.

title: Push for cash mandate to include the likes of Bunnings, McDonald's
text:
Pro-cash advocates are calling on the federal government to expand the cash mandate to big businesses including Bunnings, McDonald's and Kmart to protect its future.

Under new cash rules , most retailers that sell fuel and groceries must accept cash payments for in-person purchases of $500 or less between the hours of ﻿7am and 9pm.

However cash supporters, including ﻿Cash Welcome founder Jason Bryce, want to see more businesses included in the mandate to guarantee the future of cash for those who rely on it.

Cash supporters are calling on the federal government to expand the cash mandate. (AFR)

Small businesses with an annual turnover below $10 million are exempt from the new cash mandate, unless they share a trademark with a larger retailer.

In Australia, there were previously no laws to stop businesses from refusing to accept cash.

According to the Reserve Bank of Australia , businesses have been able to choose whether to accept cash or card payments, or both.

Advocates want to see more businesses forced to accept cash to guarantee its future. (Domain)

READ MORE: State leaders double down on refusal to make public transport free

The cash mandate will be ﻿reviewed by the government after three years to ensure it is functioning as intended.

Bryce told supporters of Cash Welcome today the mandate could easily apply to all large retailers "at the very least".

"Ideally we want all retailers to be accepting cash," he said in his latest newsletter.

"The costs of distributing cash needs to be shared by the big merchants so banks, consumers and small business don't end up paying all the costs.

"The best thing that could happen for small businesses who like to accept cash is that big businesses all support the cash system.﻿

"Big business, utilities and government agencies have escaped from this proposed cash mandate scot-free with no obligation to accept cash and support the cash system.

"Large brands like Bunnings, McDonalds, KFC, Kmart, Big W, AGL, Tyrepower, Telstra, Optus need to be captured by this cash mandate."

One Nation senator Malcolm Roberts has launched a motion to overturn the new cash rules, claiming the laws are a "back-handed" attempt to phase out the use of cash.

The sentate is set to vote on the motion tomorrow.﻿

title: Drones and apps put the future of Australian farming in young hands
text:
The stereotypical Australian farmer might be a bloke with dirt under his nails and mud on his boots, but young guns like Brooke Watts from the New South Wales central west are part of a new generation.

For the 26-year-old, it is agricultural technology (ag tech) that makes farming appealing.

"Particularly for people like myself … the practical side of farming never drew me in," she said.

"Technology plays a huge role in that space."

Brooke Watts works on her family's property in Molong, Central West NSW. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

Growing up on a sheep stud near Molong, Ms Watts saw the benefits ag tech brought to her family's farm from a young age.

Instead of spending hours on a horse or quad bike to check tanks and livestock, her dad was able to send up a drone to do the job in a quarter of the time.

"You can be so much more efficient," she said.

Ms Watts now runs an ag tech training and education business with her parents.

She focuses on bringing awareness to school students about careers in agriculture, as well as teaching farmers about the use of drones on farms.

Drones have increasingly become a staple piece of ag tech in Australia. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

"Drones have such a big spot within agriculture at the moment," she said.

"People have great benefits in spot spraying with drones, [you can] go out and check troughs … you can generate a plant health map, or a digital elevation model helps with property planning."

While Ms Watts said ag tech would never entirely replace existing practices, she said it had the ability to make farms safer, efficient, and more profitable.

Implementing ag tech on farm

Apple grower Iain MacLennan, 29, is from Orange and has also embraced the technology revolution.

He moved away to chase an alternative career as an agronomist, but returned to his family farm after learning about numerous ag tech options.

"It's about getting the records and trying to make the decisions off data, and that makes it a lot easier when you're looking at things objectively with data," he said.

Iain McLennan on his apple orchard in Orange, NSW. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

Mr MacLennan uses an app on his phone to track where he has sprayed fertiliser and pesticides.

He said that made it easier to make on-farm decisions that save both time and money.

"Everything's expensive these days, so if we can keep a record and make sure we're accurate … then it's ultimately going to lead to staying on farm for longer," Mr McLennan said.

"I don't think we are trying to reinvent the wheel, but we're definitely just trying to make that decision-making a bit easier, a bit clearer."

Iain McLennan and his family have owned the apple orchard for three years. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

Attracting younger farmers

A quarter of Australia's agricultural workforce is now under 35, according to the Australian Bureau of Agricultural and Resource Economics and Sciences (ABARES) — up from 13 per cent in 2016.

Brooke Watts travels around NSW to run workshops about the use of drones in agriculture. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

While the median age of farmers was trending upwards from 51 to 56 in 2016, it has now dropped to 50 according to the ABARES 2021 data.

Ms Watts said educating children at school about career opportunities in agriculture was vital to keep the industry relevant and thriving.

"Students love technology," she said.

" If it's something that can entice them in, there are jobs existing now that didn't exist 15, 20 years ago, so it's a really exciting space. "

Drones can be used on farms for a multitude of reasons, including monitoring, mustering and spraying. (ABC Central West: Emily Middleton)

Embracing change

Managing director at Young Aussie Farmers Zoe Carter works to attract more young people to the sector.

She said young people have been the driver for updating old practices.

"A lot of younger people are the ones behind it because they're the ones that are taking on technology, seeing these issues and going, 'How do we fix this?'" she said.

"For example, we had an automatic weighing system that would draft off sheep, so we knew how much we could feed them and how much they weighed.

"Normally, that would be done by hand."

Ms Carter said ag tech was changing the way farms operated.

"I think the older generation took a little bit longer to … accept a lot of the technologies," she said.

"You talk to any young person in agriculture, most of them are extremely passionate about the industry.

" They love it, they want to see change, they want to see things happen. "

title: Australia has not yet reached 'tipping point' for WFH directive, says expert
text:
The fuel crisis is putting pressure on workers who rely on car travel and those that meet with vulnerable clients, with fresh calls for front-line support staff to be exempt from possible fuel rationing mandates.

There are also renewed calls for more employees to work from home, though experts say the crisis has not yet reached "tipping point".

It comes after the federal government introduced a national fuel security plan on Monday and announced it would halve the fuel excise for three months, starting on Wednesday.

As a result, petrol prices will reduce by 26.3 centre per litre, a saving of approximately $19 for a 65-litre tank.

The government announced it will halve the fuel excise for three months, passing savings onto consumers. (ABC News: Chris Taylor)

Federal treasurer Jim Chalmers said people in regional and rural Australia "were front of mind" when making the decision about the fuel excise.

"One of the reasons why we landed on this approach to providing more cost-of-living relief in what we're announcing is because for people who unavoidably have to drive longer distances, the benefit will be greater," he said on Monday.

Australia's four-step plan to survive fuel crisis Fuel could be rationed and funnelled towards critical sectors such as emergency services under worst-case-scenario plan.

However, economists said while there was a short-term benefit, the underlying factors that drove up the price of fuel still remained.

"In three months, unless the measure is extended, we will observe a jump up in the price of fuel," Professor of Economics, Fabrizio Carmignani, said.

"Also, it's a measure that will benefit consumers depending on the level of consumption of fuel, and we know that typically in this situation, it's a measure that is not necessarily targeted at the more vulnerable houses."

Iran war live updates: For the latest on the Middle East conflict, read our blog.

Push for WFH directive

The oil crisis has fundamentally shifted the work-from-home (WFH) debate from a workplace culture issue to a practical way for workers to save money at the bowser and to conserve fuel stocks.

The International Energy Agency's call to limit travel and encourage remote working has given unions fresh ammunition, particularly for office-based and finance sector workers who proved during the pandemic they could work productively from home.

Wesfarmers, whose brands include Bunnings, Kmart, Target, Officeworks and Priceline, confirmed it had temporarily suspended non-essential travel for its corporate team members and discouraged flights.

The rising cost of fuel is impacting employees who rely on vehicles for their work. (ABC News: Chris Taylor)

For those with long commutes, living regionally, or with limited public transport, working from home is being framed less as a lifestyle preference and more as an economic necessity.

Aged care provider, Prestige InHome Care, will now allow frontline staff to work from home "where possible," as a direct measure to ease costs for workers.

"The cost of travel to and from work has gone up exponentially," chief executive Mark O'Brien said.

"We are confident that our organisation can continue to deliver our services working remote."

The company has also boosted rebates for kilometres travelled by frontline carers and introduced roster changes to reduce excess travel.

Professor Fabrizio Carmignani says it's a "lose-lose" situation for the government at the moment. (ABC News: Lucas Hill)

Pulling in the opposite direction is a growing resistance from some business leaders and economists who warn that Australia is not yet at "tipping point".

"The shortages we are seeing are not so critical at the moment to justify a general directive to work from home," Professor Carmignani said.

Easter getaways under threat as fuel prices soar Rising fuel prices driven by the Middle East conflict are hitting the domestic tourism sector, with cancellations surging ahead of the Easter long weekend.

"If we were to be in a situation where you go to the service station and pumps are shut down, or the moment when we see that there are very long lines, [or] the shortage is such that we need to ration the supply of fuel … that definitely could be a point where we might want to consider generalised directives.

"For some individuals, it might make sense already now to discuss arrangements with their employers."

Professor Fabrizio said even though it might seem "reactive", it was currently a "lose-lose" situation for the government no matter what direction it took.

"There is no magic wand," he said.

"We don't want to put in place directives that damage the economy beyond what is necessary based on the information that we have today."

Loading

Internationally, governments are responding to the tightening fuel supply with a mix of behavioural and workplace measures.

The Philippines has floated a four-day working week, Thailand has promoted remote work and limits on air-conditioning use while driving, while Vietnam has urged citizens to reduce travel altogether.

Unlike COVID, many workers — particularly in health, disability, cleaning, transport and trades — cannot work remotely at all, and work from home directives risks entrenching inequity between office professionals and frontline workers.

Employers are also wary of productivity losses and coordination challenges and the long- term hollowing out of CBDs.

Concerns carers unable to afford fuel

Rebecca Gray is a sole trader occupational therapist based in Perth's southern suburbs, who works with clients across homes, schools and workplaces.

She said driving an older petrol car meant fuel costs were having a huge impact on her new business.

"My practice involves direct, face-to-face therapy, which is a clinical necessity for the type of work I do," she said.

"I'm in the early stages of building my practice, so my caseload is still growing. Being flexible about where I travel is essential right now, but this crisis makes that much harder."

Ms Gray is a single parent with a mortgage, so any business costs come out of her pocket before she earns anything. (ABC News: Keana Naughton)

She said there is no telehealth substitute for the therapy and assessments she undertakes and is worried about possible fuel shortages.

"If there's no fuel at the petrol station, I can't see clients," she said.

Fears of regional health crisis as fuel costs hit rural patients The Rural Doctors Association says some patients may be forced to make "life and death" decisions due to the rising cost of fuel in regional Australia.

Angus McFarland, NSW/ACT secretary of the Australian Services Union said it was being "inundated" with calls from NDIS workers who cannot afford fuel.

The ASU has welcomed the halving of the fuel excise, which will help ease cost-of-living pressures.

"We're calling on the federal government to provide [further] fuel relief for essential disability support workers in the NDIS and we're also calling on them to make sure that community and care workers are deemed essential workers in the event that at some point there is a rationing system that comes in," he said.

Businesses step in to offer travel relief

A Melbourne-based cleaning company, that has been in operation for 25 years, said its diverse workforce, many who are single parents or students, were struggling to make ends meet.

"Many staff are in transitional phases of their lives really [with] obvious pressures," Liz Talevski said.

Managing Director Liz Talevski says the company is making small, practical measures now to help staff. (ABC News: Patrick Stone)

The M1 Cleaning Group has implemented practical measures to help staff with rising costs, including vouchers for fuel and transport, smarter rostering to reduce split shifts and carpooling and car sharing with company vehicles.

"Our workers already commute big distances with their daily activities, so any way we can help with that is a positive thing for everyone," she said.

" We don't really know what's ahead of us so practical measures, small changes, [make a] huge impact. "

Pedro Soto (left) and Yuliana Echeverry (right) have benefited from fuel and public transport vouchers from their employer. (ABC News: Patrick Stone)

Pedro Soto, who works for the company, said he made cuts to travelling or used more public transport due to the price hikes.

"It's been very difficult," he said.

"The price has increased a lot … I have to cut some travelling, I have to use more public transport.

"[The vouchers] make a very, very big difference for myself and for staff."

title: Telstra claims new coverage maps will 'make it much harder' for customers
text:
Telstra has defended its coverage as new rules are introduced to standardise mobile coverage maps across Australia.

New rules for coverage maps were introduced today by the Australian Communications and Media Authority (ACMA), following allegations from Vodafone that Telstra was "misleading customers" over the size of its mobile coverage.

All telcos will have until June 30 to publish clear maps with 4G and 5G mobile coverage and state the coverage — good, moderate, basic or no coverage.

For example, "good" coverage means consumers can expect a high-quality and seamless connectivity across voice, SMS and data communications.

Telstra's rival telco, Vodafone, has previously alleged Telstra engaged in misleading or deceptive conduct for more than 15 years, by overstating the reach of its mobile network by as much as 40 per cent.

It took its claims to the ACCC, calling on the consumer watchdog to investigate Telstra last year.

Vodafone alleges rival Telstra misled customers on mobile coverage Vodafone accuses Telstra of "misleading" consumers over the extent of its mobile coverage resulting in an "unfair market advantage."

Telstra's advertised claim that it had a "mobile network coverage area of 3 million square kilometres covering 99.7 per cent of the Australian population" had, until May last year, failed to state that the figure was based on consumers using an external antenna and booster.

In comparison, Telstra's mobile coverage without using an external antenna is significantly less, around 1.9 million square kilometres, according to analysis of figures the telco has provided to the ACCC.

Telstra's website was later updated to include clarification that no external antenna was required to access the full coverage.

New rules could 'make it much harder' Telstra argues

Group executive of Global Networks and Technology Shailin Sehgal said the telco supported the "intent" behind the new rules but had issues with the fact that any mobile signals below -115 dBm (decibel-milliwatts) would now be labelled "no coverage".

"Every month, more than 1.5 million Telstra customers use this coverage as they live, work and travel through regional and remote Australia," he said.

"This is coverage regional Australians, and those who travel through these areas, rely on.

"Nothing has changed about Telstra's network. No sites have been switched off. No coverage has been removed."

Regional Australians have previously called on Telstra to invest in better phone and internet connectivity in the regions. (Picture: ABC Southern Qld/Grace Nakamura )

Telstra is the only telco available in many parts of remote and regional Australia.

Mr Sehgal says its network "remains vastly larger with over 2,000 more mobile sites than any other network across Australia".

"Our coverage advantage in regional and remote areas is even larger," he added.

"The only thing that has changed is what will be shown on a comparable map.

"Telstra supports a single, consistent standard that helps customers compare coverage across providers, but a standard that does not permit mobile network operators to show usable coverage could make it much harder for customers to access the information they need."

Telstra boss digs in on mobile coverage claims after Vodafone challenge Telstra CEO Vicki Brady defends claim the telco provides 3 million square kilometres of coverage without using an external antenna.

In areas shown as having "no coverage", ACMA acknowledged people in some locations may still be able to make calls and send SMS, but overall service is expected to be very limited, inconsistent or non-existent.

"We have taken a considered approach, consistent with international standards, so that maps are very clear about where network availability means your service will be usable and reliable, including in an emergency," chair Nerida O'Loughlin said.

Providers may face fines for failing to comply

Telcos that breach the rules may face "enforceable undertakings, remedial directions and financial penalties", ACMA said in its announcement.

The new standards were created following a direction from Minister for Communications Anika Wells.

"People should know what they're getting when they sign up with a provider, and this improved mapping will make it easier for customers to make informed decisions about the communications services they rely on," she said.

"There are longstanding problems with the quality and comparability of mobile coverage maps published by the telcos, and the government is putting a stop to this mess.

"This will help to ensure telecommunications services are working for Australians and support our ongoing work to strengthen access to and oversight of Triple Zero."

Anika Wells has called the new maps a "win" for customers. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

She urged ACMA to continue to refine the map rules over time to include new technology.

"This is a win for Australian consumers," she added.

Ms O'Loughlin hoped the maps would help consumers make more informed decisions.

"Mobile providers make available network coverage maps, but they are measured and presented differently," she said.

"We know that consumers are frustrated that, as a result, they can't make any meaningful comparison between them.

"These new rules will ensure every carrier is giving the public a like-for-like comparison of service coverage in any location across Australia."

The maps must be updated at least every three months to reflect changes in network coverage.

Optus, Telstra and TPG must also provide their partner companies with maps for use by their customers.

title: IOC's proposed gender test not fit for purpose, says man who discovered it
text:
The genetic test the International Olympic Committee (IOC) is relying on to determine who can compete as a woman is inconclusive and should not be used in that manner, according to the scientist who discovered the key gene.

Andrew Sinclair, the deputy director of the Murdoch Children's Research Institute in Melbourne, said testing for the SRY gene was not a reliable test for determining biological sex.

Professor Sinclair discovered the SRY gene in 1990 and has continued to work on gonad development for the past 30-plus years.

The IOC said last week it was introducing SRY testing for women and girls hoping to compete at the Olympics and associated events, effectively banning transgender women from competing and limiting participation in the female category to what it called "biological females".

AOC backs IOC as concerns over testing raised The Australian Olympic Committee supports new guidelines for transgender participation at the Olympic Games, as human rights experts and LGBTQIA+ advocates raise alarm bells.

The decision means all women and girls who want to compete at future Olympic Games from LA 2028 will have to undertake the SRY test.

"Eligibility for the female category is to be determined in the first instance by SRY gene screening to detect the absence or presence of the SRY gene," the IOC said.

"The IOC considers that the presence of the SRY gene is fixed throughout life and represents highly accurate evidence that an athlete has experienced male sex development."

But Professor Sinclair said that assertion was wrong.

The IOC's policy is "foundationally based in science and led by medical experts", according to president Kirsty Coventry. (Getty Images: Andreas Rentz)

"The SRY gene alone should not determine who can compete in women's sport," he said.

"This policy is based on the overly simplistic idea that the presence of the SRY gene alone is equivalent to being male.

"Male sex is much more complex, involving multiple genes other than SRY in developmental pathways as well as hormones.

"The presence or absence of the SRY gene does not determine the range of human sex characteristics.

"Individuals with differences of sex development may carry the SRY gene but develop either complete or partial female bodies while others develop male bodies but lack SRY."

The IOC said exceptions could be made for "athletes with a diagnosis of complete androgen insensitivity syndrome (CAIS) or other rare differences/disorders in sex development (DSDs)", adding a somewhat subjective qualifier that those athletes would only be allowed if they "do not benefit from the anabolic and/or performance-enhancing effects of testosterone".

Professor Sinclair said the SRY test could only determine whether or not the gene was present.

"It does not tell you how SRY is functioning, whether a testis has formed, whether testosterone is produced and, if so, whether it can be used by the body," he said.

"The SRY gene alone should not determine who can compete in women's sport."

The IOC decision comes after boxer Imane Khelif, who has repeatedly reiterated she is not transgender, had her eligibility questioned as she won the women's 66kg gold medal at the 2024 Paris Olympics.

Paris Games gold medallist Imane Khelif revealed she tested positive to the SRY gene. (Reuters: Peter Cziborra)

Last month, the Algerian told French outlet L'Equipe she had the SRY gene.

"And it's natural," she said, adding she was "surrounded by doctors" and a professor who were monitoring her.

She said she underwent hormone therapy for the Paris qualifying event, which "lowered my testosterone levels to zero", but had faced persistent attacks from the likes of US President Donald Trump and Harry Potter author and prominent anti-trans activist JK Rowling.

New Zealand weightlifter Laurel Hubbard is the only transgender woman to compete at the Olympic Games, competing in the 87kg+ class at the 2021 Tokyo Games, failing to complete a lift.

New Zealand weightlifter Laurel Hubbard did not complete a lift at the 2021 Olympics. (Getty Images: Martin Rickett/PA Images)

World Athletics last year announced the SRY test would be the deciding factor for who could compete in the women's events ahead of last year's World Athletics Championships in Tokyo.

Writing in The Conversation after that announcement, Professor Sinclair said changes or variants in the many genes that regulated the development of a testis or ovary could result in sex reversal or a non-functioning testis or ovary.

"If there is a change in the SRY gene so it does not function as usual, then a person can fail to develop testes and be biologically female," he wrote.

"Yet they carry XY chromosomes and under the World Athletics tests [and now IOC rules] they would be excluded from competition.

"Other XY individuals may have a functioning SRY gene but are female — with breasts and female genitalia, for example — but have internal testes."

Concerns over 'sensitive' tests and false positives

In his Conversation piece, Professor Sinclair flagged that SRY testing at the 1996 Olympics in Atlanta found eight of 3,387 women's athletes tested positive — seven were resistant to testosterone and one, who had previously undergone a gonadectomy, had an apparent 5-a-steroid dehydrogenase deficiency (when someone is born with X and Y chromosomes but do not produce enough dihydrotestosterone for full male sexual development).

"With the increasing number of female participants, the cost of administering the test, including confirmation and counselling of detected individuals, was substantial with limited return," a Lancet entry about the 2002 study titled Gender Verification of Female Olympic Athletes read.

"The International Olympic Committee's Athletes Commission hence called for [the test's] discontinuation, which their executive board agreed to in June, 1999, in time for the 2000 Sydney Summer Games."

Professor Sinclair was one of a number of experts calling for that change.

"It is therefore very surprising that, 25 years later, there is a misguided effort to bring this test back," he wrote last year.

Aside from his conclusion that the test should not be used to decide if someone could compete in the women's category, he also flagged potential issues with the way the tests might be administered.

"World Athletics is recommending all women athletes take a cheek swab or blood sample to test for the presence of SRY," he wrote.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

"Normally, the sample would be sent to a lab that would extract DNA and look for the presence of the SRY gene. This may be easy enough in wealthy countries, but what is going to happen in poorer nations without these facilities?

"It is worth noting these tests are sensitive. If a male lab technician conducts the test he can inadvertently contaminate it with a single skin cell and produce a false positive SRY result."

He said World Athletics did not recognise the impact a positive result could have on someone — "which can be more profound than exclusion from sport alone" — and he called for appropriate genetic counselling to be made available.

In the recent announcement by the IOC, president Kirsty Coventry said: "Every athlete must be treated with dignity and respect, and athletes will need to be screened only once in their lifetime. There must be clear education around the process and counselling available, alongside expert medical advice."

title: Cobram Estate praises America's 'grower-first mentality' after $260m deal
text:
Australia's largest olive oil producer, Cobram Estate Olives, has completed its purchase of California Olive Ranch for almost $260 million.

Cobram Estate Olives entered a binding agreement to purchase California Olive Ranch Inc on December 24, 2025, a business responsible for about 35 per cent of the United States's extra-virgin olive oil production.

The purchase was subject to anti-trust approval from the US Department of Justice, which green-lit the sale earlier this month.

Cobram announced the successful completion of the acquisition on Thursday and stated that the company had secured a favourable exchange rate for the purchase, saving $7.1 million.

It expands Cobram's footprint in the US from about 1,400 hectares of groves to almost 3,300 hectares.

Cobram's Australian operation, which is based in northern Victoria, includes more than 2.6 million trees planted across 7,000 hectares.

Cobram co-founder and chair Rob McGavin said the company had been expanding in California for more than a decade.

"One of our major competitors came on the market, and we were able to purchase it," he said.

"Now we will have about 70 per cent of California's production."

Cobram Estate has olive groves in Boort, Boundary Bend and California. (Supplied: Cobram Estate Olives)

Policy problems

Mr McGavin said farmer-friendly water policy in California made it an attractive place to invest in irrigated agriculture.

"Certainly, their policy on water is a lot more progressive than Australia's, and a lot more friendly to farmers and thinking about secure food supply, whereas in Australia you wonder whether that's even a priority for the policy makers here," he said.

In California, only water users can own irrigation water, while in Australia, water ownership is open to all, including investors and speculators.

"That really drives the price high in periods of drought here," Mr McGavin said.

"You just don't know who you're bidding against when drought comes — it might be someone with unlimited pockets based in New York as a hedge fund.

"In the USA, it's a grower-first mentality — it's all about what makes sense for food production and farmers."

Rob McGavin says water policy in California is more farmer friendly. (Supplied: Cobram Estate Olives)

Mr McGavin said he would like to see the same policy replicated here, particularly given the large amount of water — more than 35 per cent in some irrigation zones — owned by the government for environmental watering purposes.

"I think it has to happen — I feel, in Australia's best interests, [the remaining water] should be available for consumptive users to drive industry, businesses, regional towns," he said.

In a statement, a spokesperson for Water Minister Murray Watt said there was no plan to change water ownership policy.

"Over 30 years ago, Australian governments decided to separate land and water titles to improve water use efficiency and as an economic reform to increase productivity in regional Australia," they said.

"This principle was maintained in the 2004 National Water Initiative, and it is proposed to maintain it in the new National Water Agreement between the federal, state and territory governments."

The good oil

Mr McGavin said there was growing demand for extra-virgin olive oil, and the company saw huge growth potential in the US.

According to consumer advocacy group Choice, extra-virgin olive oil is "widely recognised as being the gold standard for quality, flavour and health benefits".

Extra-virgin olive oil is renowned for its health properties. (ABC News: Heidi Sze)

Mr McGavin said extra-virgin olive oil was rated above seed oils and refined olive oil because it was extracted without heat or chemicals.

"Every day, another article comes out about how good high-quality extra-virgin olive oil is for you," he said.

In a study published in the New England Journal of Medicine, extra-virgin olive oil consumption was found to reduce cardiovascular disease risk by 39 per cent for participants with the highest levels of consumption, compared to those with the lowest levels.

From strength to strength

Michael Southan, chief executive of the Australian Olive Association, said Cobram's expansion in the US came as no surprise.

"I just think they'll go from strength because they've worked out how to [produce olive oil] so well, and the US market is a terrific opportunity," he said.

Michael Southan says there is great potential to expand the domestic olive oil industry. (Supplied: Michael Southan)

He said he also expected the Australian industry to keep expanding — despite competition for irrigation water — given domestic production only accounted for about 50 per cent of demand.

"The demand for it as a food ingredient will just continue to increase, so I'm optimistic we'll see increased investment in Australia and the industry will continue to grow," he said.

"Also, with things like the new free trade agreement with the EU, there will be opportunities there because the tariffs will come off olive oil going into Europe.

"We produce some of the best quality extra virgin olive oil in the world and … because Cobram has spearheaded the industry, they have set a very high standard for everyone else to follow, which has meant we have a very high quality product."

title: How to tell this missile image is real
text:
Huge amounts of false images and video circulating about the conflict in Iran are often making it difficult to distinguish genuine news from manipulated fakery.

ABC NEWS Verify has observed social media users calling ABC verified and published content "AI slop", "obviously fake", and even saying "photoshop much."

To help you understand how the ABC makes sure what it reports is genuine, we're going to step you through the process we took to prove one photo — an extraordinary image of a child clambering up the tail end of what appears to be a missile sticking out of the ground — was real.

It's a genuine photograph of a missile booster that has fallen to the ground after launching its payload.

This is how we know it is not a fake.

Verified agency reporting

These images appeared online four weeks ago.

Provided by the Associated Press, they were taken on March 4.

ABC NEWS Verify first geolocated them to a field near Qamishli airport in eastern Syria.

A child stands near a missile in Qamishli, Syria. (AP: Hogir Al Abdo)

Reuters also posted video of what appeared to be the same object sticking out of the ground from multiple angles on the same day, so we analysed the images together.

Both Associated Press, founded in 1846, and Reuters, founded in 1851, have long been trusted as independent news-gathering agencies, working with media organisations around the globe such as the ABC.

Loading...

In the different angles of the images and videos posted by both agencies, we established that the object is visually consistent.

The next step was witnesses. Both agencies provided interviews with local residents who claimed they saw and heard what happened.

Resident Joudi Asaad told Reuters he was herding sheep when he and others saw a missile overhead.

Another local, Hussein al Ali, told AP it was "terrifying".

"We woke up to the whistle sound of the missile. Then we came out and heard the loud hitting sound. It terrified children, women, and people in the village," he said.

Expert identifies missile piece as booster

ABC NEWS Verify then spoke with an expert to try to explain what we were looking at.

Malcolm Davis, a senior analyst in Defense Strategy with the Australian Strategic Policy Institute, said what's seen in the photo is the booster stage of an Iranian short-range ballistic missile (SRBM).

"When a ballistic missile launches, the entire missile doesn't hit the target, it carries a warhead, and it releases the warhead at a certain point to carry on to the target, the booster section is then expendable," Dr Davis said.

"And what you've seen is that the booster has launched its warhead, it's used up all its fuel, so it's come crashing down to earth, and hit the ground.

Rocket trails are seen in the sky amid a fresh barrage of Iranian missile attacks above the Israeli coastal city of Netanya. (AFP: Jack Guez)

"And because it's light, as a result of using up its fuel, it's come down in a way that hasn't created a huge impact crater; it's a small hole that basically the missiles dug itself into.

"It's not dangerous in the sense of being either an explosive risk or corrosive, [but] I wouldn't suggest you go near it personally."

Iran's missile capabilities

Dr Davis said the booster likely came from a Fateh-110 SRBM, which has been used by Iran for more than 20 years.

They have an estimated range of up to 300 kilometres.

Since the March 4 photo went viral, other similar pictures have appeared online.

A girl poses for a picture next to the remnants of a missile stuck in the ground found in Kifl Haris. (Reuters: Mohammed Torokman)

This one from March 24 shows the booster of a ballistic missile that crashed near Kifl Haris village, near Nablus, in Israeli-occupied West Bank.

title: Tim Rogers is an Aussie rock legend — that doesn't impress the bank
text:
Tim Rogers is frontman of You Am I, one of Australia's most beloved rock bands.

He's a solo artist, with a string of acclaimed albums to his name. He's been a writer, an actor, a host on radio and TV, and as of last year is a member of the ARIA Hall of Fame.

Hear Tim Rogers on the Take 5 podcast Tim Rogers has made a living in more ways than one, and his approach to living has shifted over the years. He explores what makes a good life, and what it means to be alive.

Decades in the public eye have meant he's done just about every media engagement possible, and now he finally adds a chat with Zan Rowe for Take 5 to that list.

Picking songs that revolve around the theme of "living", Rogers offered a clutch of reflections from a life in music, and insights that reveal how wide his tastes run, and how deeply he considers the world around him.

The skills to pay the bills

As the squealing guitars of Magic Dirt's 1994 song Ice fade away, Zan asks for Rogers's perspective on making a living as a musician in the current day.

The economics of music have shifted dramatically since acts like You Am I and Magic Dirt were thriving. For an artist, making one's way in the world is far more complex now.

"Everyone's got this dog barking. You need to pay rent and the electricity bills," Rogers says.

It's not just cashflow that's the issue. There's a structural inequity that those who work in non-typical industries like music must face.

"Ads [Magic Dirt frontwoman Adalita Srsen] and I were talking about real estate stuff, about just trying to get a loan. The question will come up, 'What do you do?' You say, 'We're artists' and you just get laughed out of the building.

"So, we all do performances that we wouldn't necessarily want to do. Doing the tribute shows, which I'm very glad to do, there's a little bit of a mercenary element about it."

Loading Instagram content

The tribute shows Rogers mentions are an increasingly common way for musicians from earlier eras to make a buck in an industry that fawns over fresh faces only to dump them when the next one rolls around.

Rogers himself has toured singing songs of The Rolling Stones with compadres Adalita, Tex Perkins, and Grinspoon's Phil Jamieson, and The Beatles with Josh Pyke and The Living End's Chris Cheney.

Playing other people's songs is not why they got into the business, but Rogers says it's a great way to keep afloat in lean times. He just has to remember that the paycheque is almost as important as the show.

"If I get asked to sing Rolling Stones songs or whoever the artist is, The Beatles or whatever, I think, 'Well, I love those songs. This is easy'.

"Tex [Perkins] and I, if we get asked, 'Hey, will you sing 20 Stones songs?' I go, 'Yeah, yeah, yeah.'

"They say, 'Can we tell you about what you're going to get for a performance?' It doesn't matter. I mean, this is just what we do. We kind of do it every day anyway.

" But you have to factor it in. 'Oh my God, that means I can maybe spend a couple of weeks writing and not going back to mowing lawns for a while'. "

The importance of Hourly, Daily

While the Take 5 is all about other people's songs, Zan couldn't resist asking about You Am I's third album, beloved Australian classic Hourly, Daily, which turns 30 this year.

Tim Rogers's song choices: Magic Dirt — Ice

Jake Thackray — The Bull

Joni Mitchell — Just Like This Train

NRBQ — I Want You Bad

Martha Wainwright & Linda Thompson — Or Nothing At All

Rogers credits drummer Rusty Hopkinson and Hoodoo Gurus guitarist Brad Shepherd for hipping him to the power pop that would ultimately influence the album musically.

He also says he was deeply inspired by his peers who were treading similar territory.

"There was great pop music coming out at the time, you know, bands like Swervedriver and Teenage Fanclub, where they had power, but they also had pop smarts. I just would hear that and go, 'Oh, I want that.'

"Thankfully, Andy and Russ went, 'Yeah, we'll go with that. But can we freak out at the end of every song and finish with a big calamitous things falling over?' 'Sure'."

"It wasn't really the style of the time to write something like that. But that's what the band were kind of listening to. Pop music and power pop."

You Am I and Friends play Hi Fi Way Watch You Am I perform tracks from their classic second album Hi Fi Way with a little help from Sarah Blasko, Hockey Dad's Zach Stephenson and more.

The album's depiction of suburbia is as visceral as it is vivid — neighbours coughing up their lungs, cabbies telling you not to use the mirror, chip shops, milk bars and the complex relationships happening beyond manicured suburban lawns.

"It's a pretty strange record," Rogers reflects.

"We did 200-and-something shows the year that we wrote that record and I was missing home.

"I guess I just needed something to keep my fragile little noggin together where we were doing these endless tours of the States. So, it was Patrick White books and Gerard Manley Hopkins poetry and definitely Kinks records."

Rogers's day‑to‑day reality was miles from the quiet suburban scenes he wrote. The writing perhaps offering a calmer place to slip into amid the whirlwind he was living.

"I refer to it as our Sullivans record," he says. "It's all about tea and toast, when really it was kind of party drugs and hard alcohol.

"But where I went to for my own peace of mind was the Sullivans aspect of Australian living. It was just where I went to when I was in a hotel in St Petersburg, Florida, desperately wanting some peace."

The only thing that matters

The way a person lives — their priorities, their whole sense of the world — can shift when children arrive.

Rogers's daughter, Ruby Rogers‑Garcia, released her first album last year with her band, Ruby and the Clumsy Dollies.

"She came out to Australia for the Hall of Fame time and doing her own shows. I was the bass player in her band and it was terrifying. I love those songs so much," Rogers says.

Loading Instagram content

As he plays the final song of his Take 5, Martha Wainwright singing Linda Thompson's Or Nothing At All, Rogers says he's only now beginning to understand unconditional parental love. That there's no right or wrong way to live when one is happy and safe.

"One night we probably had a couple of shandies and Ruby extrapolated upon, you know, 'What do you want from me?' And I thought … 'Absolutely nothing, just your existence.'

"I remember my mother saying to me as a kid, because I was mucking up a lot and really didn't have a lot of prospects, she said to me one time, 'I actually just want you to be happy.'

"I think I read too much late-19th-century Russian literature where the expectations are so great on the kids and to become something respectable.

"Ruby just happens to be brilliant, but I just want her to be happy, you know? She could be all these things or nothing at all."

Tim Rogers plays shows in Canberra, Newcastle, Sydney and Milton through April.

Hear the Take 5 podcast any time on ABC listen.

title: Government to decide on Gold Coast quarry bid after public backlash
text:
The Queensland government will make the final decision on a controversial planned quarry in a Gold Coast suburb, following months of community opposition.

State Planning Minister and Deputy Premier Jarrrod Bleijie has announced his decision to exercise call-in powers, deciding on whether to approve or knock back the plan out of the Gold Coast City Council's hands.

The quarry, which had been proposed to the council last year by building material giant Boral, received strong opposition from the community, as well as state LNP MPs Ros Bates, Laura Gerber and Hermann Vorster, who represent nearby electorates.

The state government on Tuesday stepped in to announce it would consider exercising special decision-making powers before the city's council could debate the development application.

Boral operates sites across more than 300 locations in Australia. (ABC Illawarra: Penny Burfitt)

Mr Bleijie said he received more than 2,900 submissions about the project and believed the call-in would help provide certainty for concerned residents.

"We will calmly and methodically consider both the merits and impacts of this project and deliver certainty for everyone involved," he said.

Member for Mudgeeraba Ms Bates said she would keep her community informed in the coming weeks.

"I know how strongly people feel about this proposal. I feel the same," she said.

Public backlash ahead of decision

Before the decision, Boral executive general manager Paul Noakes said the company had seen a "coordinated social media campaign" that spread misinformation about the proposal.

"We do respect the public has a right to express their opinion, but we ask that it's actually done anchored to facts," he told the ABC in February.

"Some examples were dust mitigation, noise mitigation, I even saw some commentary around a [200-hectare] quarry."

He said the actual development application sought to operate on 26 per cent of the land holding in the area, equivalent to 56 hectares.

The pitch comes as the predominant quarry at West Burleigh nears end-of-life, with Boral previously arguing a Reedy Creek quarry was needed to help source materials to build much-needed homes on the Gold Coast.

Councillor Glenn Tozer said he believed there was "sufficient supply" to meet demand over the coming decade.

"But no doubt Boral will take a different view and suggest that potentially this resource is essential to their business and to be able to build things they're being asked to build," he said.

Cr Tozer said the state government call-in closed the door to any future appeals in the Planning and Environment Court, where council decisions can be appealed.

"What the public can be sure about is that when the state minister makes a decision about it, it is a final decision," he said.

Mr Bleijie will decide whether the project proceeds or is rejected within 30 business days.

title: Anonymous $200,000 donation helps free dinner service as demand doubles
text:
It's Monday night in the heart of Gosford on the NSW Central Coast — the air is warm and a DJ blasts out 90s dance music.

Community volunteers unwrap large silver trays of hot, home-cooked food, ready to be served up to anyone who wants a free feed.

Hot chicken pasta, fried rice, salads and garlic bread are on the menu, and the queue is long — mums with young kids, older couples, teenagers, the homeless, as well as those who have just knocked off work, all waiting patiently, paper plate in hand.

This locally grown outreach service in the CBD's main park has been offering help to people in need every Monday night for five years — and lately demand is soaring.

Volunteers homecook and deliver the weekly hot meals to Kibble Park. (Supplied: Safelink Alliance)

Until recently, this weekly volunteer service was only just surviving financially — so the people behind it were shocked when they were handed a $200,000 anonymous donation.

"Literally, we were speechless," said Safelink Alliance financial officer Michael McQuade.

" We just looked at each other and went, 'Are you kidding me?' "

Safelink Alliance founder Saia Latu said the charity was sustainable thanks to the "game-changing" donation. (ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince)

Safelink Alliance founder Saia Latu, who was named Central Coast Council's Community Champion last year, was always confident the charity would "be able to last", but this was a game-changer.

"I was working on keeping the service going week to week," he said.

" Now we're looking at growing the service. It's amazing. "

Changed fortunes

Mr Latu said the benefactor became aware of the Monday night meals through a mutual acquaintance and made the donation to "help us be sustainable in the community".

The group was struggling without a food van, needed to store and transport supplies and equipment, after its old one broke down.

"We thought it was support for the van, then they turned around and said, 'Any van you want plus the total of $200,000 over three years.'

" [It was] like winning the lottery. "

Saia Latu's vision is to offer free meals five days a week. (ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince)

A chance to give back

Volunteers are also on the rise, with about 60 people part of the monthly roster.

For Kayden, one of the newest recruits, being involved was extra special because he and his partner were once among those lining up for the meals they now serve.

The pair had just moved to the region, with no friends or family support and were struggling to pay the bills.

Volunteer numbers for the outdoor meal service have almost doubled in recent years. (ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince)

"It wasn't just the free meal, it was hanging out with people constantly every week, and [them] checking up on you," Kayden recalled.

"They say a lot about how bad it is, but you don't really know until you've gone through it yourself.

" We just want to give back to the community — to put some good back in. "

And Kayden also vouched for the meals.

"The food is amazing," he said.

"[We've] never had a bad comment, so obviously it's always great."

The dream

With money in the bank and a new van in the pipeline, the next challenge for Safelink Alliance is how it can better meet the growing demand for its services.

According to local support services, homelessness is at a crisis point on the Central Coast due to rising living costs, low rental availability and a serious shortage of affordable housing.

A food pantry of donated goods is also available to anyone who needs it. (ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince)

Between 60 and 80 people roll up for a meal each week, about double the number a few years ago.

"We don't discriminate; there's no criteria to come and sit with us," Mr Latu said.

" For the first time ever, we're preparing to hit triple numbers with 100 people within the space of two hours on the Monday night. "

The Community Champion's dream is "to go to five services, Monday to Friday" along with an outreach arm, connected with local services.

"It's pretty much cookie-cutting this, hopefully building into other regions (on the Coast), but doing it better."

Volunteers ready to serve a free meal to anyone who wants a feed. (ABC Central Coast: Mary-Louise Vince)

Mr McQuade also dreamed of securing a premises "where we can cook and can provide … something like a community kitchen and food hub with pre-made meals.

"Times are only getting tougher, and this service is only going to get busier," he said.

The Monday night service was initially launched by Care4Coast in 2020 but was taken over by some of the volunteers, who officially established SafeLink Alliance in 2025.

title: Five illegal fishing vessels burned at sea amid ABF crackdown
text:
The Australian Border Force has destroyed five fishing boats confiscated from illegal foreign fishers in the course of its crackdown on the practice in Queensland's north.

The ABF launched an operation to deter illegal fishing in February, following the interceptions of 10 boats since the start of the year.

Since the operation launched, the ABF said it had intercepted a further five boats, all of which have now been destroyed at sea.

Two of the vessels were stopped while fishing illegally near Albany Island at the tip of Cape York on February 20, with a third vessel intercepted in the Escape River on Cape York Peninsula on March 3.

Two more illegal fishing craft were intercepted off Trochus Island four days later.

Illegal fishing craft are either destroyed at sea or escorted out of Australian economic waters. (Supplied: ABF)

Of the 19 boats in total intercepted this year, nine have so far been destroyed, with the other 10 escorted out of Australian waters.

In a statement, the ABF said destroying vessels was a "key measure" of enforcement and a deterrent to illegal fishing operations.

Farmers fear disease outbreak from illegal fishing Australian farmers are concerned illegal foreign fishing vessels spotted off the Queensland coast could carry dangerous pests and diseases.

"If you enter Australian waters to fish illegally, you will be detected and intercepted, and the consequences can be immediate — your vessel can be seized and disposed of under Australian law," acting deputy commander of the Maritime Border Command Brooke Dewar said.

"Increased vessel destruction through Operation BROADSTAFF reflects a deliberate enforcement posture."

ABF personnel on the water consult with the Australian Fisheries Management Authority to decide whether to destroy detained vessels at sea, or escort them out of Australia's economic zone.

"Vessel destruction is one tool. Seizure of fishing equipment, education and escorting vessels out of Australian waters are also critical parts of a multi-layered enforcement approach," Deputy Commander Dewar said.

153 illegal fishers have been prosecuted in the Darwin Local Court since July last year. (Supplied: ABF)

Crews taking part in illegal activity are transported to Darwin for processing through the courts, before being repatriated.

153 Indonesian fishers have been prosecuted in the Darwin Local Court since July last year.

The ABF said recently detained vessels had been intercepted before any illegal fishing had occurred.

"We will continue strong enforcement in the Torres Strait for as long as necessary," Deputy Commander Dewar said.

title: Fair Work Commission abolishes junior pay rates for young adults
text:
Young adults working in retail, fast food and pharmacies are set to receive a pay rise, after a decision by the Fair Work Commission described as "up there with the introduction of equal pay for women in the 1970s".

The commission moved to abolish junior pay rates for young adult employees while maintaining them for minors, but the staged changes won't come into effect until December.

Hearing from more than 80 witnesses across the three industries, it determined that employees aged 18 to 20 should no longer be subject to "discounted" junior rates.

Terri Butler, deputy president of the Fair Work Commission, said in making the decision, it considered whether there was any difference in the value of work performed by junior employees and other employees in the same classifications under the same awards.

"We have been particularly interested in the extent to which junior rates serve or do not serve the interests of children and young people," she said.

"Young teenagers who are trying to get their first job usually wanting to balance work with secondary education, can benefit from being able to accept discount rates compared with older people doing the same job."

Terri Butler is the deputy president of the Fair Work Commission. (AAP: Darren England)

It decided not to vary the rates for employees who are still minors under 16.

"They are in a position of particular vulnerability and greater labour market disadvantage," Ms Butler said.

"We consider that, among the other matters we've taken into account, there are strong fairness reasons for allowing them to continue to accept discount rates, to get their start in the workplace, gaining valuable experience."

Under the changes, it is estimated around half a million workers will be eligible for the pay rise, according to ABS data.

The ruling addresses an application to vary junior rates under the General Retail Industry Award, the Fast Food Industry Award, and the Pharmacy Industry Award.

There will be a phase-in period of up to four years and the first wage adjustments are scheduled to begin in December.

'Landmark decision'

"Junior pay rates" applied to people below the age of 21, meaning 18-year-olds were paid 70 per cent of the award rate, 80 per cent for 19-year-olds and 90 per cent for 20-year-olds.

Under the commission's ruling, the rate for 18-year-olds will increase by 5 per cent each year until 2029, bringing it in line with an adult wage.

Larger businesses previously claimed the case would have a "totemic" impact to the structure of employment.

Big employers like McDonald's and Coles are often stepping stones to full-time employment, with Woolworths alone providing about one in eight Australians with their first job.

Woolworths alone provides about one in eight Australians with their first job. (ABC News: Simon Tucci)

Advocates have argued youth should be paid adult ages because you can enlist in armed forces at 17, and can vote, drive, drink alcohol and smoke from 18.
